- Basil Hess

**Date and time:** Tuesday, April 14, 2026, 10:00–10:30 US Eastern / 7:00–7:30 US Pacific / 16:00–16:30 Central European

Information about the OQS project is available at [https://openquantumsafe.org](https://openquantumsafe.org). The project dashboard is at [https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html](https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html).

## OQS Technical Steering Committee

### TSC co-chair (issue [#246](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/246))

Michael has nominated Rodrigo Martín to be TSC co-chair and eventual chair. Douglas welcomed the proposal; other TSC members are invited to review the discussion on the issue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Open a vote on the TSC co-chair nomination in the next couple of days.

### PR [#264](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/264) — Add Hart to the security-managers team

The PR would give Hart visibility into CVEs. It has two approving reviews, and Michael confirmed his earlier concerns have been addressed, so it is ready to land.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Merge this PR.

## liboqs

Douglas reviewed the 0.16.0 priorities. Progress is being made on the algorithm support levels table (issue [#2045](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2045)); an offer of help is still outstanding for Douglas to follow up on, but the issue should be resolved relatively soon.

### HQC update (PR [#2328](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2328))

Bruce reported that most of the code changes are ready; he is working through remaining CI pipeline failures, after which he will raise the PR for review. The change is expected to be moderate in size and relatively straightforward to review.

Rodrigo noted that HQC is disabled by default in the oqs-provider's list of algorithms, so the provider configuration will need to be updated accordingly when the liboqs 0.16.0 release lands.

### PR [#2384](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2384) — Fix reading beyond buffer end

Norm confirmed the PR is just awaiting review; Douglas will check on the remaining review comment after the call, and it is expected to be ready to land shortly.

### PR [#2396](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2396) — cuPQC ML-KEM derand symbol names

Bruce flagged this PR as urgent: it fixes a compile failure for any user trying to build liboqs with the CUDA implementations of ML-KEM, since liboqs currently references symbols that do not exist. The code change is also non-trivial, so additional review would be appreciated. Agreed to tag the PR for inclusion in 0.16.0.

### FrodoKEM release notes

Rodrigo pointed out that when both FrodoKEM variants were included, the meaning of the existing algorithm name changed — the same algorithm name now refers to a different algorithm. This behavioral change was discussed in the relevant issue but was not captured in the PR or documented as release-notes material, and it should be clearly called out for downstream consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Open an issue and PR to update the liboqs 0.16.0 release notes to highlight the FrodoKEM algorithm-name behavior change.

### liboqs governance

Michael noted that liboqs [GOVERNANCE.md](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/blob/main/GOVERNANCE.md) is significantly out of date and should be rectified before a release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Open an issue to review and update the liboqs governance file, and include it in the 0.16.0 scope.

### PR [#2403](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2403) — MAYO update

Basil reported that this PR pulls a MAYO update from upstream fixing a reported issue. It should be straightforward to merge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Tag PR [#2403](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2403) for inclusion in the liboqs 0.16.0 release.

### PQClean wind-down and algorithm support levels

Rodrigo observed that 0.16.0 is likely the last liboqs release before the PQClean end-of-support deadline. The algorithm support levels table (issue [#2045](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2045)) will document which algorithms (Classic McEliece being the most prominent example) continue to rely on PQClean, so that interested parties can step forward to maintain them or propose alternative implementations. Douglas confirmed he added a note to issue #2045 linking back to last week's discussion.

Michael suggested that, in addition to per-algorithm maintainer information, the table include a top-line fallback statement: if none of the listed maintainers can be reached for a serious (security) issue, users should know whom to contact (e.g., the security mailing list / TSC chair / Hart). For non-security issues, pointers to discussions are sufficient.

## OQS-OpenSSL provider

### Security advisories (PRs [#766](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/766), [#767](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/767), [#768](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/768))

Rodrigo reported active work this week on two security advisories that were opened about three weeks ago. In his judgement none of them rise to the level of actual CVEs — they were double-free and error-path leak issues — and PRs addressing them (submitted by the same reporter) have all been merged. Douglas confirmed Rodrigo can close the advisories directly via the GitHub UI, leaving a comment so the reporter is aware; the reporter is already engaged via the PR discussions.

### PR [#655](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/655) — Build as shared library

Rodrigo reviewed the stale PR. The idea is interesting but the PR as drafted would make shared-library builds the default, which he does not think is appropriate and would change how many users consume the provider. He will draft a conservative alternative that adds shared-library builds as an opt-in, disabled-by-default option, so users who want it can explicitly enable it while preserving existing behavior.

### PR [#641](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/641) — generate.sh / python scripts cleanup

Rodrigo looked at this PR, which concerns issues encountered by the author of the security-advisory PRs when running the script. There is some mismatch with how the code/CI runs the format, but he believes at least part of the PR is worth reviving and will follow up.

### PR [#752](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/752) — Preserve property string for KEMs

Still stale. Norm reported no update; part of the difficulty is that the relevant data structure is opaque, so it is hard to easily assert that the fields were updated as desired. Rodrigo now leans toward the suspicious behavior being an OpenSSL issue rather than an oqs-provider bug, but it currently cannot be reproduced without Norm's PR code, since that PR is the only place in oqs-provider that translates the property query down to the classical component. Michael reiterated his earlier suggestion to separate the two concerns: construct a minimally oqs-provider-independent reproducer of the suspected OpenSSL behavior and raise it with Richard Levitte. Rodrigo agrees but does not yet see a way to replicate the behavior outside Norm's PR. Consensus: the PR has enough uncertainty that it should not be part of the next oqs-provider release.

## OQS-OpenSSH

Alex had nothing new to report since last week and has not had time to pick up the already-filed items.

## OQS-BoringSSL, oqs-demos

No updates.

## ci-containers / armhf

Basil reported that PR [#101](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/ci-containers/pull/101) relates to the weekly armhf tests failing in liboqs (issue [#2401](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2401)): the armhf builds fail due to a missing dependency in the CI container, and the underlying bullseye image itself is end-of-life. The practical choices are to deactivate the armhf builds or migrate to another image.

The group saw no compelling consumer use case for armhf. Michael noted that armhf is currently listed in tier 2 in liboqs `PLATFORMS.md`, and that if it is dropped from CI the platforms file must be updated accordingly. Agreed sequence: first open a PR demoting armhf from tier 2 to tier 3 in `PLATFORMS.md` (higher visibility), and, assuming no objections, then remove it from the weekly CI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Open a liboqs PR demoting armhf from tier 2 to tier 3 in `PLATFORMS.md`, and, pending no objections, follow up to remove armhf from the weekly CI.

## Language wrappers

No updates.

## liboqs 0.16.0 outlook

Douglas closed by summarizing that the focus over the coming weeks is to chip away at the identified 0.16.0 items, draw a line in the sand on scope, and move through the release cycle. Help with reviews and landing PRs is welcome; members should flag anything else they consider important for inclusion soon.

## Next meetings

The next status meeting is on Tuesday, April 21, 2026, at 12:30 PM US Eastern (6:30 PM Central European). Two weeks from now (April 28) at this slot will be the next OQS Technical Steering Committee meeting.
